section.title: Send Out Your Light and Truth
section.sectionRationale: The conclusion of the single poem begun in Psalm 42, sharing its identical closing refrain. Here the downcast soul turns from remembered anguish to active petition, asking God to vindicate him and to send out light and truth to lead him home to the altar of God, his greatest joy.
section.thesis: Still opposed by an ungodly nation and deceitful men, the psalmist asks God to vindicate him and to send out His light and truth to guide him back to the altar, resolving the whole poem's downcast refrain in the settled hope that he will yet praise his Savior and God.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the plea for vindication and for God's light and truth to lead the way home, verses 1-4; and the closing refrain of hope, verse 5, identical to Psalm 42:11, resolving the shared poem.

units.label: Send out Your light and Your truth

verses.text:
{{shaphat|Vindicate}} me, O God, and plead my case against an ungodly nation; 
deliver me from deceitful and unjust men.
For You are the God of my refuge. Why have You rejected me? Why must I walk in sorrow because of the enemy's oppression?
Send out Your light and Your truth; let them lead me. Let them bring me to Your holy mountain, and to the place where You dwell.
Then I will go to the altar of God, to God, my greatest joy. I will praise You with the harp, O God, my God.
Why are you downcast, O my soul? Why the unease within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise Him, my Savior and my God.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, vindicate, or plead a legal case. The psalmist asks God to act as his advocate and judge, formally clearing his name and pleading his cause against those who wrong him, language of the law court applied to personal distress.

basic.context: Continuing straight on from Psalm 42 without a separate heading in the Hebrew manuscripts, this psalm moves the same downcast soul from remembered anguish into active petition. The psalmist asks God to vindicate him against an ungodly nation and deceitful men, and prays that God's own light and truth would go out ahead of him like guides, leading him back to the altar, his greatest joy, where he will again praise with the harp.
basic.hermeneutics: Read as the conclusion of one continuous prayer with Psalm 42, this psalm shows lament properly resolving not in the removal of every circumstance but in renewed petition and a settled resolve to praise, the same refrain now bringing the whole poem to its close.
basic.originalLanguages: The opening plea, shaphat, vindicate, is law-court language, asking God to act as judge and advocate, pleading the psalmist's case formally rather than merely offering private comfort. Light and truth are personified, sent out like messengers or guides to lead the way home.
basic.typology: The plea to be vindicated against unjust accusers and led by God's own light and truth to His dwelling place anticipates Christ, who was Himself unjustly accused yet vindicated by God in the resurrection, and who is Himself called the light of the world, John 8:12, and the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6, leading His people to God's presence.
basic.christ: The psalmist prays that God would send out light and truth to lead him to the altar of God. Jesus declares Himself to be exactly this: 'I am the light of the world,' John 8:12, and 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me,' John 14:6. What the psalmist asked God to send, light and truth as guides home, God sent fully and finally in the person of His Son, who also, like this psalmist, was unjustly accused yet vindicated by God through the resurrection.
basic.god: God is shown as both refuge and judge, the one to whom the wronged bring their case for vindication, and the one whose own light and truth, sent out like living guides, alone can lead the soul home to worship.
basic.crossReferences: John 8:12 records Jesus declaring Himself the light of the world. John 14:6 records Jesus declaring Himself the way, the truth, and the life, leading to the Father. Romans 8:33-34 shows God as the one who vindicates and justifies His people against every accusation.
basic.oneStory: The psalmist's plea for vindication and for God's light and truth to lead him home to worship anticipates Christ, who is Himself the light and the truth, unjustly accused yet vindicated by the Father, now leading all who trust Him to the true dwelling place of God's presence.

context.literary: Forms one continuous poem with Psalm 42, sharing its identical refrain, verse 5 matching 42:11 word for word, and completing the movement from remembered anguish to active petition and resolved hope.
context.historical: Likely composed alongside Psalm 42 by a temple singer or Levite separated from Jerusalem, now praying actively for vindication against those who oppress him and for God to guide his actual return to the holy mountain.
context.canonical: Some ancient manuscripts and traditions treat Psalms 42 and 43 as a single psalm without a separate heading, and their shared refrain has shaped Christian liturgy, notably the introit prayers of many historic Western liturgies, 'Judica me, Deus,' Vindicate me, O God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To move the downcast soul from remembered anguish into concrete petition, asking God for vindication and for His light and truth to guide the way back to worship, resolving lament in settled, active hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the psalmist's particular plea to return to Jerusalem's temple; it prescribes petitioning God actively for guidance and vindication as the pattern for moving from lament toward renewed worship in any season of opposition or distance from God.

debates.question: Should Psalm 42 and Psalm 43 be read as two separate psalms or one continuous poem?

views.label: They should be read as one continuous poem, since they share an identical refrain, a coherent progression of thought, and some Hebrew manuscripts present them without a separate heading for Psalm 43
views.case: The refrain in 42:5, 42:11, and 43:5 is word for word identical, and Psalm 43 lacks its own superscription in the Hebrew text, both strong indicators of original unity.

views.label: They should be read as two related but distinct psalms, since the Masoretic textual tradition and most manuscripts number and divide them separately, allowing each to be studied for its own emphasis
views.case: The traditional Hebrew canon and nearly all manuscript traditions number them as separate psalms, and Psalm 43 does introduce a new emphasis, active legal petition for vindication, that develops beyond Psalm 42's remembered anguish.

debates.bearing: Whether read as one poem or two, the shared refrain shows they were composed as a matched pair meant to be read together, with Psalm 43 completing the movement Psalm 42 begins, from downcast memory to active petition and resolved praise.

study.originalLanguages: The plea shaphat, vindicate, is courtroom language, asking God to judge and plead the psalmist's case formally. Light and truth, personified as guides sent out ahead, picture God's own character going before the psalmist to lead him home to the altar.

typology.type: Light and truth sent out to lead the psalmist to God's dwelling place, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who declares Himself the light of the world, John 8:12, and the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6.

typology.type: The plea for vindication against unjust accusers, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own vindication by the Father through the resurrection after unjust accusation and condemnation.

study.christ: The psalmist prays that God would send out light and truth to lead him home to the altar. Jesus takes up both images directly of Himself: 'I am the light of the world; whoever follows Me will not walk in darkness,' John 8:12, and 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me,' John 14:6. And where the psalmist sought vindication against an ungodly nation and deceitful men, Christ Himself was unjustly condemned yet vindicated by the Father in His resurrection, becoming the light and truth that leads every downcast soul home to God's presence.
study.god: God is revealed as both refuge and righteous judge, the one to whom the wrongly accused bring their case, and the one whose own light and truth, sent out like living guides, alone lead the soul home to worship and joy.

crossReferences.note: Jesus declares Himself the light of the world, fulfilling this psalm's plea for God to send out His light.
Jesus declares Himself the way, the truth, and the life, fulfilling this psalm's plea for God's truth to lead the way home.
God is shown as the one who vindicates and justifies His people against every accusation, echoing this psalm's plea for vindication.

study.oneStory: The psalmist's plea for vindication and for God's light and truth to guide him home to worship anticipates Christ, who is Himself the light and the truth, unjustly accused yet vindicated by the Father, now leading all who trust Him into the true and lasting presence of God.

sermon.title: I Will Go to God, My Exceeding Joy
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Psalm 43, tracing how the psalmist moves from vindication and God's sent-out light and truth toward the settled resolve to go to God Himself as his exceeding joy, a pattern for moving from downcast lament to renewed worship.

quiz.q: What legal language does the psalmist use to open his plea in verse 1?

options.text: He asks God to vindicate him, using courtroom language of judging and pleading a case.
options.note: Shaphat pictures God acting as judge and advocate, formally clearing the psalmist's name.

options.text: He asks God to forget the whole matter entirely.
options.note: The plea is for active vindication and a pleaded case, not for the matter to simply be forgotten.

options.text: He asks his human accusers to judge him fairly.
options.note: The plea is addressed to God, not to the accusers themselves, asking God to plead his case against them.

options.text: He asks for a delay in any judgment.
options.note: The plea is for active, prompt vindication, not for delaying judgment.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to send out in verse 3?

options.text: His light and His truth, to lead him to God's holy mountain and dwelling place.
options.note: These are personified as guides sent ahead, an image Jesus fulfills directly in John 8:12 and John 14:6.

options.text: An army to defeat his enemies.
options.note: The specific request is for light and truth as guides, not military force.

options.text: Wealth to compensate for his losses.
options.note: The request concerns guidance home to worship, not material compensation.

options.text: A message of comfort to his friends.
options.note: The request is for personal guidance to God's presence, not a message directed at friends.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist say he will do once he reaches the altar of God, verse 4?

options.text: Praise God with the harp, calling Him his greatest joy.
options.note: Arrival at the altar is tied directly to renewed, joyful, musical praise.

options.text: Offer a formal complaint against his accusers.
options.note: At the altar the response described is praise and joy, not further complaint.

options.text: Remain silent out of exhaustion.
options.note: The text explicitly describes active praise with the harp, not silence.

options.text: Ask for permission to leave again immediately.
options.note: The tone describes God as his greatest joy at the altar, the opposite of wanting to leave immediately.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's closing refrain in verse 5 relate to Psalm 42?

options.text: It is word for word identical to the refrain in Psalm 42:11, showing the two psalms form one continuous poem.
options.note: This shared refrain, appearing in 42:5, 42:11, and 43:5, is strong evidence the two psalms were composed as a unified prayer.

options.text: It directly contradicts everything said in Psalm 42.
options.note: The refrain is identical, not contradictory, showing continuity rather than contradiction.

options.text: It introduces an entirely new and unrelated theme.
options.note: The refrain repeats the same words used in Psalm 42, rather than introducing something new.

options.text: It is found nowhere else in the Psalter.
options.note: While the refrain is distinctive to this shared poem, the point is that it recurs identically three times within Psalms 42-43, not that it appears nowhere else at all within these two psalms.

quiz.q: How does Jesus take up this psalm's image of light and truth sent to guide the way?

options.text: He declares Himself both the light of the world, John 8:12, and the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6.
options.note: Jesus applies both images from this psalm directly to Himself as the one who leads people to the Father.

options.text: He rejects any association with light or truth in His teaching.
options.note: Jesus explicitly and repeatedly claims both titles for Himself in John's Gospel.

options.text: He applies the image only to the Holy Spirit, never to Himself.
options.note: While the Spirit is also called the Spirit of truth, John 14:6 and John 8:12 have Jesus applying these titles directly to Himself.

options.text: He applies the image only to John the Baptist.
options.note: John the Baptist testified to the light, John 1:8, but Jesus explicitly claims the title of light and truth for Himself.

questions: The psalmist asks God to vindicate him against unjust accusers rather than defending himself. Where do you need to entrust your reputation or vindication to God rather than fighting for it yourself?
The psalmist prays for God's light and truth to be sent out ahead of him like guides. What decision or season of confusion in your life right now needs God's light and truth to lead the way?

Verse 4 calls God 'my greatest joy.' Would you honestly describe God, rather than any other person, achievement, or comfort, as your greatest joy right now? What would need to change for that to be true?

This psalm and Psalm 42 share the same refrain, showing lament resolving not by denying pain but by repeated, active choice to hope. What refrain of truth do you need to preach to your own soul repeatedly this week?
Jesus declares Himself both the light and the truth this psalmist longed for. How does praying this psalm differently, knowing Christ Himself is the answer to its plea, change how you pray it?

translationNotes.note: Vindicate renders shaphat, courtroom language for judging or pleading a legal case, asking God to act as advocate and judge on the psalmist's behalf.
Light and truth are personified as messengers or guides sent out ahead of the psalmist, an image Jesus later applies directly to Himself in John 8:12 and John 14:6.
This refrain is identical to Psalm 42:11, strong evidence that Psalms 42 and 43 were originally composed as one continuous poem.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 43:1-5. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org, confirming John Piper's message on Psalm 43.
